# engineer-of-source experience — Marix

## Workflow runtime

- The active hierarchy is Task → Intent → Step → Invocation/Relay. Intent owns plain Plan state; there is no active Plan actor or protocol namespace.
- Register every child actor in its task-wide registry before `start`. On termination, commit lifecycle result/status and complete the registry entry before notifying the owner; reversing either order creates lookup races.
- `Lifecycle` stores `ActorStatus<Result>` atomically. Parent updates carry the terminal child result; use registry result lookup only for dynamic reconstruction.
- Task uses a current-thread Tokio runtime driven by a dedicated standard thread. Spawn child actors through that shared runtime; blocking the task dispatcher prevents every nested actor from progressing.
- `TaskAccess` permits construction/insertion only. Keep lookup, completion, cancellation, lineage checks, and routing in `TaskRuntime`; retain its weak `SessionContext` link.

## Model and tool boundaries

- Deepseek is stream-only. Buffer SSE tool-call fragments by index, require a valid terminal `[DONE]`, validate complete JSON-object arguments, then emit one normalized `StepDraft`; never execute partial deltas.
- 2026-07-20: DeepSeek chat-completion payloads explicitly disable Thinking in the base payload because Thinking rejects `tool_choice`; keep forced tool relays on `tool_choice: "required"` and non-tool relays on JSON response format.
- Invocation validates the exact registered tool name and JSON arguments. Return validation and unknown-tool failures through normal typed results so parent actors cannot strand.
- Windows managed processes launch executables directly with explicit arguments. Validate UUID paths, reject symlinks, bind ownership to PID plus creation time, bound output pages, and retain the lock file.
- 2026-07-21: Native web search invokes DuckDuckGo, Yahoo, and Wikipedia through curl with a 60-second per-attempt `--max-time`; preserve provider fallback order and retry settings together.
- 2026-07-21: A model backend can be fully self-contained (e.g. `src/server/model/glm/`): own its own `XConfig` struct inside `backend.rs` instead of `common/config`, take it directly in `new(config)`, and stay unreferenced except for the two mirrored lines in `model/mod.rs` (`pub mod x;` / `pub use x::XBackend;`). A `pub use`-exported but never-constructed backend does not trigger a dead-code warning under `cargo check`, so an intentionally-unwired sibling backend compiles clean without `#[allow(dead_code)]`. GLM's chat-completions API is OpenAI-compatible SSE, so its stream accumulator mirrors Deepseek's byte-for-byte (same five-tool-call cap, same `stop`/`tool_calls` finish_reason values, same `thinking: {"type":"disabled"}` payload field).
- 2026-07-21: Deepseek's and Glm's near-duplicate stream/request/payload logic was consolidated into `src/server/model/openai.rs` (`pub(super) struct OpenAiCore`, sibling-scoped so `deepseek`/`glm` submodules can use it but nothing outside `model` can). Each backend now holds `core: OpenAiCore` and delegates `ModelBackendImpl`/`Debug`. Provider-specific text (log/error strings) is threaded through a `provider: &'static str` field/param instead of being hardcoded per backend — this is the general pattern for extracting shared logic behind a `pub(super)` core type when two+ sibling modules are byte-for-byte identical except for one label.

## Rust language gotchas hit during refactors

- When a private helper takes two-or-more reference-shaped parameters (e.g. `provider: &str` plus `value: Option<&serde_json::Value>`) and its return type borrows from only one of them, elision fails with E0106 ("missing lifetime specifier") because 2+ input lifetimes make the borrow source ambiguous. Fix by naming an explicit lifetime tied only to the parameter actually borrowed from, e.g. `fn f<'a>(provider: &str, value: Option<&'a Value>) -> Option<&'a [Value]>` — leave the unrelated parameter's lifetime elided/independent.
- If a `#[derive(Clone)]` struct gains a field whose type does not itself derive `Clone`, the derive fails at the *call site* (`Backend: Clone` bound not satisfied), not at the field's own definition — when wrapping shared state (e.g. `OpenAiCore`) inside multiple `Clone`-deriving wrapper structs, add `#[derive(Clone)]` to the shared type itself. `reqwest::Client` is `Arc`-backed internally, so cloning it is cheap and safe to do per-request/per-task.
- Clippy's `too_many_arguments` (default threshold 7) can appear as a *new* warning purely from splitting one bundled config struct into N free function parameters during a refactor, even when total information carried is unchanged. Prefer cloning the owning struct (`self.clone()`) into a single owned local and passing that one value into the spawned task/helper, over decomposing it into many scalar parameters — this is usually also a more faithful read of "use `self.field`"-style refactor instructions.
- The Rust coding style doc's ~500-line soft-file-size guideline can be explicitly overridden by an explicit task instruction to consolidate into "a single file, not a subdirectory" — when the two conflict, the explicit, more specific instruction wins; note the override rather than silently splitting the file back up.
- 2026-07-21: On this workspace's cargo version, `cargo fmt --check --workspace` errors ("unexpected argument '--workspace'" — it wants workspace args after `--`); use `cargo fmt --all --check` instead to format-check every workspace member.
- 2026-07-21: A "discriminator + N mandatory payload sections" config shape (e.g. `selected: String` plus always-required `deepseek: DeepseekConfig, glm: GlmConfig`) is strictly simpler to deserialize than a data-carrying enum: every section is a plain non-`Option` field, so a missing section surfaces for free as serde's own "missing field" error (bucketed under the existing `ConfigError::Toml` variant) — no custom `ConfigError` variant or manual validation needed. When replacing a data-carrying enum with this shape, `RawXxxx` intermediate-deserialization structs typically become unnecessary too: derive `Deserialize` directly on the real public struct, and reserve `#[serde(default = "...")]`/`#[serde(skip, default = "...")]` only for the few fields that are genuinely optional-with-a-computed-default (e.g. `core: CoreConfig` via `#[serde(default = "default_core_config")]`, `system: System` via `#[serde(skip, default = "System::new")]`) or need post-`toml::from_str` path resolution (e.g. `runtime`, `tool.directory`) — `build_config` then collapses to one `toml::from_str` call plus a couple of targeted field mutations, not a full manual struct reconstruction.
- 2026-07-21: A `grep` pattern using a long `|`-alternation group inside a quoted-string literal match (e.g. `"name": "(Config|RuntimeConfig|...|CONFIG_FILE)"` with 10+ alternatives) can return zero matches even though the content is present and a simpler single-term grep for one of the same alternatives succeeds immediately after — root cause not fully diagnosed (possibly an alternation-count limit or literal-parenthesis/quote escaping interaction). Workaround: prefer short, simple patterns (2-4 alternatives, or one distinctive substring) over long enumerated alternations when grepping JSON/text metadata.
- 2026-07-21: When a chain of two uncommitted tasks does "design A landed, then a follow-up task fully undoes/replaces design A with design B" without an intervening commit, `git diff` against the last real commit shows only the *net* structural delta — any type/field that was added by the first task and removed by the second never appears in the diff at all (as if it never existed). This is a good sanity check for design-metadata status decisions: if a `design.json` element was marked `added` (not `modified`/`renamed`) because "no prior evidence of it existing," a clean `git diff` against HEAD showing that same identifier as a fresh `+` addition (with no corresponding `-` removal of a same-named-but-different-shaped prior version) corroborates the call.

## Transport and configuration

- Remoc setup must keep its connection future driven after returning channels. Abort guarded connection tasks on timeout/rejection; otherwise failed handshakes leak workers.
- Server channel listeners bind the IPv4 wildcard independently of `server.ip`; connectors resolve the configured address. Server-driven host registration sends session identity and tool query before publishing the channel.
- Install a remote telemetry sink only after TCP, remoc, and token acceptance succeed. Startup telemetry exhaustion is fatal rather than a silent local fallback.

## Repository invariants

- Keep public protocol re-exports synchronized between `protocol/lib.rs` and its mirrored `protocol/mod.rs`.

## Design-metadata (`src_meta/`) nuances

- 2026-07-21: `pub(super)` counts as "outward-facing" for `design-json-update` element-tracking purposes, same as `pub`/`pub(crate)`/`pub(in ...)` — a `pub(super) fn` gets its own tracked element even though it never appears in the crate's genuine public API surface.
- 2026-07-21: When a private function is renamed and the *old* name was never itself tracked as a design.json element (e.g. it only became `pub(super)`/tracked-worthy as part of the same change that renamed it), classify it as a net-new `added` element at its new name — not a `renamed` element — because there is no prior element identity to rename in place. Reserve `renamed`/rename-in-place strictly for identifiers that already had their own tracked element under the old name.
- 2026-07-21: `src/common/structure/tests/channel.rs` (any path under a `tests/` folder, at any depth) gets zero design.json treatment — it is not scanned for elements and its changes never appear in any folder's status arrays, confirmed via both the `changed`- and `full`-mode path rules.
- 2026-07-21: Files/folders directly under `src/common/structure/` are covered by two design.json layers at once: `src_meta/common/structure/design.json` (native depth) and, one level up, `src_meta/common/design.json` — the latter carries a mirrored copy of the same `structure`-owned elements one layer down (per the "elements = own layer + direct child modules one layer down" rule), so both files must be edited together and kept in sync whenever a `structure/*.rs` element set changes. `src_meta/design.json` (repo root) only needs `modified: ["."]` plus purpose/child-summary wording when the change is a pure descendant with no direct file under `src/` itself.
- 2026-07-21: The "own layer + direct child module one layer down" mirroring rule is applied inconsistently pre-existing across the repo — confirmed by directly comparing three real files during one `config.rs` rewrite: `src_meta/common/config/design.json` (own-layer, depth-3) tracks public structs/enums plus `CONFIG_FILE` but *not* private free functions or the other private statics (`CONFIG_ENV_VAR`, `CONFIG_CACHE`); `src_meta/common/design.json` (mirror one level up) tracks the *same* structs/enums/`CONFIG_FILE` **plus** every private free function and both other private statics; `src_meta/server/design.json` (one level above `src/server/relay`) mirrors **nothing** from `relay`'s elements at all — just a `childModules` purpose-summary entry, no element list. When a change touches a deeply-nested file, always check the immediate-parent design.json's *existing* convention for that specific child (does it mirror elements at all? functions too, or just structs/enums?) rather than assuming one of the three patterns — do not try to harmonize them, per the skill's own explicit warning that conventions are inconsistent.
- 2026-07-21: An embedded test-fixture TOML string that lives inside a `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block *within* an ordinary source file (e.g. `src/server_telemetry/server.rs`'s fixture, not a separate `tests/`-folder file) is not literally covered by the skill's "ignore folders named tests" rule, since it's not a folder — but the established repo precedent (confirmed by checking that an equivalent prior-task edit to the same fixture left that file's design.json completely untouched, `changeStatus: "unchanged"`, no top-level `modified` array at all) is to treat it the same way: skip design.json edits entirely for changes confined to an in-file `#[cfg(test)]` module, since test code isn't itself a tracked source element and the containing function's own `codeSegments` range doesn't extend into the test module.
- 2026-07-21: Promoting a dependency already resolved transitively (e.g. `socket2` locked at `0.6.4` via `tokio`) to a direct dependency at a compatible version constraint produces a minimal `Cargo.lock` diff — just one line added to the crate's own dependency list, no version/checksum churn elsewhere. Not a sign something is wrong; expected and cheap to verify with `git diff -- src/Cargo.lock`.
- 2026-07-21: `cargo fmt` will reflow a hand-written multi-line `.map_err(|e| ...)` closure or a multi-arg logging call into whatever line count it prefers (sometimes collapsing, sometimes expanding) — always run `cargo fmt -p <crate>` and re-`--check` after hand-editing non-trivial expressions from task prose rather than trusting the pasted formatting verbatim.
- Logging from deep inside `src/common` submodules (e.g. `structure/channel.rs`) uses `use crate::Logger;` (re-exported at the crate root), the same convention already used by `src/common/actor/actor.rs` and `runtime.rs` — check crate-root re-exports before assuming a fully-qualified module path is needed.
